topic: cooking — how to make a decent stew for the week

turns.content: right so I need a stew recipe that isnt going to be a total disaster. something I can make on sunday and eat for like 4 days. whats the best approach.

A big batch beef stew is probably your best bet for a cook-once-eat-all-week situation. Here's a solid, simple one:

**Ingredients:**
- 1kg stewing beef (chuck is ideal), cut into chunks
- 3-4 carrots, chunked
- 3-4 potatoes, cubed
- 2 onions, diced
- 2 cloves garlic
- 500ml beef stock
- A tin of chopped tomatoes
- Tablespoon of tomato puree
- Worcestershire sauce, a few splashes
- Bay leaf, thyme
- Salt, pepper, flour for dusting

**Method:** Dust the beef in seasoned flour, brown it in batches in a hot pan with oil. Set aside. Soften the onions and garlic, add everything back in with the stock and tomatoes. Low oven (150°C) for 2.5-3 hours, lid on.

Keeps brilliantly in the fridge and actually tastes better on day 2. Do you have a preference for what kind of meat, or is beef the plan?
beef is fine. I can get it cheap from one of the farms so thats not an issue. the issue is ive made stew before and the meat ends up like shoe leather. every time. what am I doing wrong.

Classic problem, and it's almost always one of two things:

1. **Heat too high.** If the stew is boiling away rather than gently simmering, the meat seizes up and goes tough. You want barely-a-bubble, not a rolling boil. That's why the low oven method works so well -- it's more even than the hob.

2. **Not cooking long enough.** Stewing beef has a lot of connective tissue. It goes tough first (around 1-1.5 hours) and then breaks down and goes tender (2.5-3 hours). If you're pulling it at the tough stage thinking it's done, that's your problem.

So: low and slow. 150°C, 3 hours minimum. Don't rush it. The meat should practically fall apart when you press it with a fork.
aye thats prob it then. ive been doing it on the hob on like medium heat for an hour and a half and calling it done. no wonder its terrible.

Yeah, that's your culprit. An hour and a half on medium is basically the worst of both worlds -- hot enough to toughen everything up, not long enough to break it back down.

Switch to the oven, drop the temperature, and double the time. You'll notice a massive difference. The gravy will be thicker too because it reduces properly over that time.

If you don't want to have the oven on for 3 hours, a slow cooker is brilliant for this. Chuck everything in, set it to low, leave it for 8 hours while you're out. Come back to a perfect stew.
dont have a slow cooker. might get one tbh they seem like the kind of thing thats made for people who cant be bothered cooking properly. which is me.

Honestly, a slow cooker is the best investment for someone who wants decent food with minimal effort. You can get a perfectly good one for about £20-30. No skill required -- just throw things in, set it, and forget it.

They're also great for batch cooking, which sounds like what you're after. Stews, chillis, curries, pulled pork -- all basically the same technique. Dump ingredients in, go to work, come home to food.

The only thing they're not great at is browning meat, so you'd still want to sear the beef in a pan first for flavour. But that's 5 minutes of effort and then you're done.
aye alright ill look into it. what about veg. I usually just do carrots and tatties but its a bit boring after 4 days of the same thing.

Good call -- variety helps a lot when you're eating the same stew all week. Some good additions:

- **Parsnips** -- they go sweet and soft, really good in a beefy stew
- **Swede** -- holds its shape well, adds a bit of earthiness
- **Celery** -- not exciting on its own but adds depth to the base
- **Leeks** -- melt down beautifully, very Scottish-appropriate
- **Mushrooms** -- add them in the last hour so they don't go to mush

You could also vary what you eat it with across the week. Day 1-2 on its own, day 3 over mashed potato, day 4 with some crusty bread or dumplings. Same stew, different experience.
dumplings. never made them. are they difficult.

Not even slightly. They're basically the easiest thing you can make:

- 150g self-raising flour
- 75g suet (the shredded stuff in a box, not actual kidney fat)
- Pinch of salt
- Enough cold water to bring it together -- maybe 6-8 tablespoons

Mix, form into golf-ball-sized lumps, drop them on top of the stew for the last 25-30 minutes with the lid on. They steam and puff up. Done.

You can add herbs to them too -- thyme or parsley mixed into the dough. Takes about 2 minutes of actual work.
right thats actually doable. even for me. the suet thing in a box — is that atora or whatever its called.

Yeah, Atora is the one. It comes in a red box for beef suet and a blue box for vegetable suet. Either works for dumplings -- the beef one has a slightly richer flavour but honestly the difference is marginal.

You'll find it in the baking aisle, usually near the flour. Lasts ages in the cupboard too, so you can just keep it there for whenever you fancy dumplings.

One tip: don't overwork the dough. Mix it just until it comes together and then stop. If you knead it too much they go dense and heavy instead of light and fluffy.
sound. right ill give this a go on sunday. stew in the oven for 3 hours, dumplings on top at the end. if it goes wrong im blaming you.

Fair enough, I'll accept full responsibility. But I'm fairly confident you'll be fine -- it's genuinely hard to mess up if you keep the heat low and give it enough time.

Remember: brown the meat first, low oven at 150°C, 3 hours, dumplings in for the last 30 minutes. You'll have a week's worth of food sorted. Good luck with it.
